author: @snowman333-
text: you could use a spring set up for furling/unfurling similar in fashion to the tail on a windmill water pump for remote stock tank needs. In higher winds, the tail folds over to the side causing the bladed part to be positioned with its own rotational axis at 90° to the wind direction effectively shutting down and protecting the moving parts. When the wind speed drops, the springs pull the tail back at 90° from the bladed section's axis.

author: @brotolero
text: great solution to a problem that not exist. Savonius turbines are specialy adecuates to survives to high winds, because his TSR is around 1. The more clever idea to solve the  situation with a storm wind is a PMG with 2 or 3 stators,  only one can be activated in normal winds and the second and third stator can close the circuit when the voltage  produced ( = rpm of the turbine) exed certain limits. The only problem with this is the cost of the extra magnets and copper for the extra stators, but by the whole cost of a turbine not is extremely significant if you search for maximum eficiency in all conditions. If the wind is more like a twister, your last worry will be the survivance of a turbine.... The technical complexity of the "armony" wind turbine is the main reason because this company are like 7 or 8 year promising his turbines and never were produce nothing for sale,  only protoypes. Theys promote this design for catch the attention of litlle investors and other naives peples to get his money. But the design is like i said previously a solution for a non-existent problem. And its solution causes the blades to be so complex to produce and maintain that they are no longer a design to scale in large productions.

author: @zylascope

author: @kaupoveegen543
text: One remark - this is actually not Savonius design - as the scoops end are closed. Savonius'es basic idea was that there must be the cap between the overlapping scopes, allowing wind blow "throw" it, thus producing also additional torque due to lift, not only drag difference.

author: @JoshebMangru
text: Sir, I might seem like I'm stupid, what if a funnel is made from light weight materials and placed in front of the turbine to funnel the wind, at a higher speed, to the turbine. This funnel will have to be mounted in such a way that is aligns itself with the flow of the wind direction. When you walk from one building to the next, the space between those buildings have lots of wind speed.

author: @Digidoc316
text:
The Harmony design allows the turbine to spin consistantly at it's optimum speed for an extended period, adapting to the verying wind speeds.
In addition, the Savonius develops a significant torque, necessary to overcome startup cogging and full load resistance of the alternator.
The strong torque allows for the gearing up of the alternator to acheive usable generation speeds.
Coupled with a low-speed, high output alternator, it becomes quite an effective pairing.

author: @Illisil
text: 3d printed back in the 1960s wow

author: @tetleyk
text: The whole "efficiency" thing is a bit of a red herring. A more reasonable measure of the generator would be to look at how many turbines you can fit per hectare and then look at the cost, maintenance, wind speed range per megawatt. For example, these turbines do very well packed close together whereas HAWTs do not. In short, it doesn't matter if the efficiency is only 30% if you can get 5000% more in a given area than for HWATs.

author: @101jchristensen
text: A lot of the critical comments here focus on similar ideas using mechanicalcentrifigal speed governor or furling  solutions. They seem to disregard the MASS these solutions introduce to the sail mass, which ideally should be zero. This design, using a single motor/gear solution, avoids some of this mass.  Correct me, please, if i am wrong?

author: @101jchristensen
text: In a mechanical engineering lab exercise in the 80s we are given a small DC motor to use as a generator and a budget of $10 and tasked to create the most efficient wind generator (power/cost). We found a beer can on the road, cut it in half vertically, offset the halves and glued them to the motor shaft. It only lasted a few seconds in front of the test fan. But it did produce some voltage, and our cost was zero, so we   won. Hail Savonius!

author: @salmanhendershot782
text: While watching this I had the idea of a flyball governor. Couldn't a flyball governor be used to provide the closing force to furl them? I would think that with some clever engineering you could also use the governor to tune it to provide a specific (and therefore optimal) rotation speed of choice. It would also reduce the complexity of the design overall and provide safety due to the mechanical nature of the governance.

author: @SwampYankee-o4b
text: These claims that a Savonius wind turbine is coming in at 30% efficiency has absolutely no data to support it. The owner of Harmony  turbines who has absolutely no prior experience in wind turbine design has also made this false claim. It’s been 100 years of Savonius turbines, we have seen many companies come and go utilizing the Savonius design. I don’t believe the math has changed regarding the efficiency of a drag style turbine, perhaps the only thing that has changed is peoples gullibility as the next generation of people don’t know any better.

author: @russellzauner

author: @gerryplayz4532
text: They should have had regenerative brakes on them but I think capture and redirection to a contained turbine makes more sense than a big lump of something flying around. I hear people complain about noise, light flicker and pressure waves from the current wind turbines, now they reckon the pressure waves are why whales and odd stuff are washing up on shore?

replies.author: @__WJK__
replies.text: Actually, it's unlikely that "pressure waves" are the cause of "whales and other odd stuff washing up on shore" given such events have been occurring all over the world for 1000s of years a/k/a marine animals get sick, injured and/or die of old age all the time, just like other animals...except when marine animals die...some occasionally get washed up on beaches before scavengers devour them, or they wash up shore before they've had a chance to sink to the bottom of the ocean. Side note/fun facts... there have been indications of Sperm Whale strandings as early as 14,000 years ago in southern Spain, and the earliest confirmed/documented mass stranding of whales occurred in 1897 in the Falkland Islands...long before modern inventions were around to create things like pressure waves, or military/commercial sonar/radar began operating. The actual "suspected" cause of whale beachings, is believed to be caused by fluctuating magnetic currents within the Earth and/or strong magnetic fluctuations from solar storms which likely interferes with how whales and other migratory animals navigate.

author: @saiello2061
text: In the early days I asked Mr Moore about basic power data when he'd got to the stage of running his substantially metal constructed wind turbines on the top of pick up trucks. Surprisingly he said he didn't have ANY data up to this relatively late stage because he was waiting on electrical expertise from some university. I said you only need install a simple Prony brake. He didn't know what a Prony brake was. I left it there....... It's a visually impressive design but like many, I suspect that's all it'll be in the end. There are far better ways for controlling for high wind speeds than the complexity of a folding wind turbine.

replies.author: @Longbowgun
replies.text:
It would work but it would have to have a fixed height for the maximum opening.
Because of the massive amount of torque you could generate with a very long but small diameter set of these turbines it would be a challenge to find material that could withstand the force without shearing. Perhaps one could add a gear to the end to increase the RPM of the generator. Would that lessen the load?

author: @jimbobarooney2861
text: I came up with a similar idea back in the mid nineties while completing my enginnering degree, my idea had only two sails like the standard savonius rotor, they slid past each other (no hinges), some weights would close the sails when the wind speed increased, springs were incorported to close the sails, and a damper to even the movements.

replies.author: @mememaster147
replies.text: That's exactly what I thought when I found out how it operated, they could do the same job with just weights and springs.

replies.author: @__WJK__
replies.text: Interesting, though I think you're confusing "dampen" with damper, that or maybe auto-correct is picking the wrong word(?)

replies.author: @jimbobarooney2861
replies.text: @__WJK__ damper or (dashpot in engineering parlance)

replies.author: @jimbobarooney2861
replies.text: Another idea i had was to make the sails telescopic like either a bellows, (concertina partition wall) or possibly have moving solid C shape solid sail that folded into each other vertically, again the swept area would increase in lower wind speeds and decrease in higher winds as need be

replies.author: @jimbobarooney2861
replies.text: @__WJK__ damper (dashpot)

author: @steveknight878
text: Instead of changing the turbine's geometry with increased windspeed, would it not be possible to do some sort of gearing on the generator - some method of increasing the number of generators being turned -  so that as the wind speed increases, the power coming out of the generator(s) increases, without much change in speed?  Perhaps a generator where the number of connected coils increases with wind speed?

replies.author: @lafamillecarrington
replies.text: They do have a system for altering the gap between the magnets and coils which has this effect, allowing the rotor to spin at very low wind speeds.

author: @onbedoeldekut1515
text:
The perfect wind turbine design would be a vertical cylinder array of Fibonacci cones all directing their spouts onto a central scoop turbine.
Because of the focused flow of wind from any direction, any flow will be multiplied exponentially because there's nowhere for it to go other than out the end of a thin spout.
I had initially considered a chrysanthemum shaped array for the pure beauty, but that would be far too inefficient.

author: @John_B55
text: Drag type rotors do suffer less with strong winds as they always turn at a rate less than the wind speed. Aerofoil designs turn faster than the wind so tend to be more reliant on governors. In a domestic scale setting, Savonius style should probably be fine up to hurricane force.

replies.author: @tonyaltobello6885
replies.text: No

replies.author: @gregfetisov936
replies.text: @tonyaltobello6885why?)

replies.author: @jimbobarooney2861
replies.text: If you make it robust enough, it will withstand a hurricane, (but maybe not cost effective) it works mainly on drag forces, this force will increase at the rate of the windspeed squared.  Savonius rotors also try to "walk" basically the magnus effect, a lateral force to the wind direction

replies.text: @tonyaltobello6885 yes it can if it can furl into a cylinder which already is more aerodynamic

author: @armandbourque2468
text: There's a wind responsive turbine design, to moderate energy capture. Max capture in low wind, progressively reducing with wind speed. Used on a stand alone refuge shelter in the alps.

replies.author: @armandbourque2468
replies.text: I beleive it operated on centrifugal force and weights to close up totally in high winds, and a spring return to default fully open in low winds. The trick is in balancing the forces of wind, weight/speed, and spring return. Quite elegant, simple tobuild, maintain and repair, once the above formulae are established, and analogue.

author: @justtinkering6713
text: Your statement that it turns quite fast when it's fully open is not correct. It actually spins faster when it closes up to a smaller diameter, just like an ice skater pulling their arms inward. I feel it's an over complicated design rather than very simple. You can achieve furling much simpler and you maintain speed and power with the design I have on my ch.  Just hang a Savonious turbine on a cable and let it furl itself, no computer or electronics or motors required.
